sections.heading: Why Cats Need Scratchers
sections.content:
Scratching isn't destructive behavior — it's essential maintenance. Cats scratch to shed old claw sheaths, stretch their back and shoulder muscles, mark territory (paw glands leave scent), and relieve stress.

Cats that don't have appropriate scratching surfaces redirect to furniture, carpets, and doorframes. The solution isn't declawing (which is painful and causes behavioral problems) — it's providing surfaces that satisfy the same instincts.

The key is matching material, orientation, and placement to your cat's preferences. We tested 10 scratchers to find the ones cats actually prefer.

sections.heading: Best Sisal Scratching Posts
sections.content:
Sisal rope is the gold standard scratching material. Its rough, fibrous texture provides ideal resistance for claw maintenance. Most cats prefer sisal over carpet, cardboard, or wood.

**Key features for sisal posts:**
- **Height**: 32"+ so cats can fully extend while stretching
- **Base weight**: Heavy enough that the post doesn't wobble or tip
- **Sisal quality**: Tightly wound, thick-gauge natural rope
- **Post diameter**: 3.5"+ for stability

Sisal posts last 2–3 years before needing replacement (you'll see loose, frayed fibers hanging off). Some posts offer replaceable sisal wraps.

See our [cat scratching post collection](/collections/best-cat-scratching-posts) for tested picks.

sections.heading: Scratcher Placement Tips
sections.content:
Even the best scratcher fails if placed in the wrong location. Cats scratch when they wake up, after eating, and when they want to mark territory.

**Optimal placement:**
- Next to furniture they currently scratch (redirects the behavior)
- Near sleeping areas (cats scratch after waking)
- At room entrances (territory-marking locations)
- Near cat trees (complements vertical climbing)

Never hide scratchers in unused rooms. Cats scratch in social areas to communicate with their household.

faq.question: What material do cats prefer for scratching?
faq.answer: Most cats prefer sisal rope, followed by corrugated cardboard. Carpet is least preferred and can teach cats that carpet elsewhere is acceptable to scratch.

faq.question: How tall should a cat scratching post be?
faq.answer: At least 32 inches — tall enough for an adult cat to fully extend vertically while scratching. Shorter posts don't allow the full stretch cats need.

faq.question: How do I train my cat to use a scratcher?
faq.answer: Place it near furniture they currently scratch. Rub catnip on it. Gently drag a wand toy across the surface. Praise and treat when they use it. Never force their paws onto it.

faq.question: How often should I replace a cat scratcher?
faq.answer: Sisal posts: every 2–3 years. Cardboard scratchers: every 2–4 months (or when deeply shredded). If your cat stops using it, it may be too worn.

faq.question: Do cats prefer vertical or horizontal scratchers?
faq.answer: It varies by cat. Most cats like both. Provide at least one vertical post and one horizontal/angled surface so your cat can choose.

faq.question: Will a scratcher stop my cat from scratching furniture?
faq.answer: In most cases, yes — if placed correctly (next to the furniture they scratch) and made from a preferred material (sisal or cardboard). Cover furniture temporarily with double-sided tape while training.
